sections.title: Phrasal verbs
items: **bring up** — take care of a child until he or she becomes an adult
**fall out (with)** — have an argument with sb and stop being friends
**go out with** — be the boyfriend/girlfriend of

exercises.title: Topic vocabulary (gap fill)
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with words from the box.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with words from the box.

items.sentence: You should ...... to Max for forgetting his birthday.
items.explanation: You should say sorry → **apologise** to Max.

items.sentence: Don't open the door to a ...... when you're home alone.
They live in a busy ...... near the station.
My uncle is very ...... and often pays for everyone's meal.
I'm really ...... to my neighbours for helping us move house.
She's in a cheerful ...... because her best friend is visiting.
You should always ...... other people's private opinions.
We're very ...... friends and we tell each other everything.

exercises.title: Circle the correct word
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.originalSentence: Jack wants to be **independent / ordinary** and pay his own rent.
items.completedSentence: Jack wants to be independent and pay his own rent.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "independent" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "independent" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "ordinary" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.ordinary: "ordinary" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("and pay his own rent.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: their flat before the guests arrived.
items.originalSentence: They decided to **decorate / recognise** their flat before the guests arrived.
items.completedSentence: They decided to decorate their flat before the guests arrived.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "decorate" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "decorate" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "recognise" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.recognise: "recognise" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("their flat before the guests arrived.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: I can always **trust / rent** my cousin with a secret.
items.completedSentence: I can always trust my cousin with a secret.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "trust" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "trust" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "rent" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.rent: "rent" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("my cousin with a secret.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.originalSentence: Can you **introduce / defend** me to your new neighbour?
items.completedSentence: Can you introduce me to your new neighbour?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "introduce" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "introduce" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "defend" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.defend: "defend" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("me to your new neighbour?").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: before, but Dad says he's my uncle.
items.originalSentence: I've never met that **relation / guest** before, but Dad says he's my uncle.
items.completedSentence: I've never met that relation before, but Dad says he's my uncle.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "relation" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "relation" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "guest" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.guest: "guest" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("before, but Dad says he's my uncle.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

items.sentenceAfter: , but they are still good friends.
items.originalSentence: My parents are **divorced / private**, but they are still good friends.
items.completedSentence: My parents are divorced , but they are still good friends.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "divorced" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "divorced" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "private" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.private: "private" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence (", but they are still good friends.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

exercises.title: Phrasal verbs (complete the gaps)
exercises.instructions: Write the correct form of the phrasal verb in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the correct form of the phrasal verb in each gap.

items.sentence: My parents worked hard to ...... three children.
I don't want to ...... Emma over a silly argument.
items.explanation: Have an argument → **fall out with** Emma.

items.sentence: Do you ...... your brother well?
items.explanation: Have a good relationship → **get on with** your brother.

items.sentence: Zoe used to ...... Daniel, but they aren't together now.
items.explanation: Be in a romantic relationship → **go out with** Daniel.

items.sentence: I promised to help, so I don't want to ...... anyone.
Can you ...... the dog while we're away?
items.explanation: Take care of → **look after** the dog.

exercises.title: Prepositional phrases (matching)
exercises.instructions: Match sentence beginnings with the correct ending.
exercises.studentInstruction: Match sentence beginnings with the correct ending.

items.sentence: She cooked the whole meal by ...... .
My best friend and I have a lot in ...... .
I'm still in ...... with some friends from my old school.
They are completely in ...... with each other.
items.explanation: Romantic phrase → in **love** with each other.

items.sentence: I didn't hurt your feelings on ...... .
He lives on his ...... in a small flat.

exercises.title: Word patterns (adjectives, verbs and nouns)
exercises.instructions: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).
exercises.studentInstruction: Match each sentence beginning (1–8) with the correct ending (A–H).

items.sentence: I'm very fond **D** of my grandparents.
items.explanation: **Fond of** is the correct pattern — we feel fond **of** grandparents.

items.sentence: Leo is jealous **A** of his sister's success.
Please be kind **F** to the new student.
My aunt is married **B** to a musician.
We're proud **E** of our cousin's achievements.
She apologised **C** for being late.
They argued **G** about money again.
I admire him **H** for his honesty.

exercises.title: Word formation
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with the correct form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: I have great ...... for people who always help others. (ADMIRE)
It took a long time, but she finally offered him ...... . (FORGIVE)
I value ...... more than anything in a friendship. (HONEST)
The teacher made a short ...... before the new student spoke. (INTRODUCE)
Moving away from home gave him a lot of ...... . (CONFIDENT)
Nobody believed him because everyone knew he was a ...... . (LIE)
Her ...... to stay calm is very useful in difficult situations. (ABLE)
Their ...... improved after they talked honestly. (RELATE)

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "on my own" — alone.

wrongOptions.A: "by yourself" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "on my own" is correct.
wrongOptions.by yourself: "by yourself" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "on my own" is correct.
wrongOptions.B: "in contact" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "on my own" is correct.
wrongOptions.in contact: "in contact" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "on my own" is correct.

wrongOptions.on purpose: "on purpose" does not match the grammar or meaning clue in this sentence — "on my own" is correct.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Eliminate options that break grammar first, then check which remaining word fits the meaning.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "on my own" is the only option that matches both meaning and register in this sentence.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The context points to "lie to" — tell untruths.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Time expressions like for, since, already, and yet are strong present-perfect signals.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links past actions to now — look for markers like for, since, already, yet, or ever.

exercises.title: Mixed revision
exercises.instructions: Complete the sentences with one suitable word.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the sentences with one suitable word.

items.sentence: I don't want to argue ...... my parents tonight.
items.explanation: Argue **with** someone (not argue about parents here).

items.sentence: They ...... up after a long relationship.
He's in a bad ...... because he lost his phone.
Can you ...... me to your cousin?
items.explanation: Meet formally → **introduce** me to your cousin.

items.sentence: It was very ...... of you to help the new student.
items.explanation: It was **kind** of you = you were kind.

items.sentence: I didn't say that on ...... . It was an accident.
They rent a small ...... near the university.
I keep in ...... with my old neighbours.
